TABLE II PER-CLASS PERFORMANCE ON THE VALIDATION SET OF WAYMO OPEN MOTION DATASET.

Fig. 1. FlowS architecture. Scene inputs are processed by a Transformer-based encoder (Agent/Map). The Learned Prior module replaces the standard Gaussian base by producing K scene-conditioned anchor trajectories. During training, the decoder (Dθ) learns flow dynamics from anchors to ground-truth distributions via a step-consistent displacement loss (Lstep). At inference, a single forward evaluation produces multi-agent trajectory predictions, filtered by NMS.

Fig. 2. Why a learned prior helps one-step flow matching. Left: Gaussian start. A scene-agnostic base sample Z ∼ N (0, I) can lie far from the target future, so a single update must both identify the correct mode and traverse a long displacement, which increases error. Right: learned prior. Scene-conditioned anchors Ak already lie near plausible futures, so one-step CFM only needs a short corrective transport to reach the target. The prior therefore improves conditioning by reducing transport length, yielding ∥YGT −Ak∥ ≪ ∥YGT − Z∥.

Fig. 3. Step-consistent displacement field. Left: the base CFM velocity field vϕ(Xt, t, c) along the transport path from anchor A to the target. Middle: semigroup consistency enforces that one 2d update matches two composed d updates. Right: at inference, the displacement field sψ(A, 0, 1, c) realizes the full transport in one pass, while preserving the infinitesimal limit limd→0 sψ = vϕ.
